author: jsign
content:
There is a currently pending situation that must be resolved in the specs, which is returning `stateless_validation=False` if the bytes received as input can’t be SSZ-deserialised. Currently, the guest program panics and the current strategy is to lean towards `stateless_validation=False` to have a clear path failure.

The twist in this case, is that although we could set `stateless_validation=False` , [StatelessValidationResult](https://github.com/ethereum/execution-specs/blob/projects/zkevm/src/ethereum/forks/amsterdam/stateless.py) also has `new_payload_request_root` and `chain_config` fields which we can’t meaningfully set since we couldn’t even deserialize the input.

I think there are at least two obvious options:

- Option 1: Set `stateless_validation=False`, `new_payload_request_root=0x00..`  and `chain_config = all-fields-zero-values`
- Option 2: redefine `new_payload_request_root` and `chain_config` as optionals, and serialize these as SSZ List with max length 1. (Or an extra variant is create a wrapper to only have a single Optional that inside has those two fields).

This situation of an invalid SSZ would be very weird, and I can hardly imagine those proofs even being sent to the network, but we should still define it. Maybe option 1 is fine, since not overcomplicating the SSZ API with a case that probably won't happen. I’m planning to resolve this case *somehow,* since we should — we can also lean on an option now and reconsider later, ofc; just opening this if anyone has any strong opinion or preference.

author: kevaundray
content: If we always check the statelessValidation Boolean before doing anything else, then would option 1 be fine?

author: kevaundray
content: So in general, if the Boolean is false, then the chain config etc can be anything

author: jsign
content: Yep. If you first check the boolean, then there is no ambiguity on how to interpret the other two values.

author: 0xrubo
content: we currently have problems with exception handling, so our guest panics in such cases. that's the cause of those 12 eest tests failures -- execution-wise things are alright, just for some invalid scenarios it panics instead of returning a status of failure

author: jsign
content: Ah, sounds good. I think prob that problem catching panic on your side might be something solvable? (i.e. prob related with many of hte compilation magics you have to do for .net ? )

author: 0xrubo
content: it is solvable, but at the cost of performance. something to contemplate about internally.
